sections.heading: Symptoms
sections.content: Penile squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) most commonly presents as a palpable, visible lesion on the penis. The lesion may be associated with pain, discharge, bleeding, or a foul odor, particularly if the patient has delayed seeking medical treatment. Morphologically, the lesion can be characterized as nodular, ulcerative, or fungating. A critical aspect of the presentation is that the lesion may be obscured by phimosis, which can hinder both visual inspection and physical examination. Patients may present with signs of more advanced disease, including palpable inguinal lymphadenopathy and/or constitutional symptoms such as fatigue and weight loss. The primary lesion is most frequently located on the glans (34.5%), prepuce (13.2%), or shaft (5.3%).

symptoms.name: Palpable/visible penile lesion
symptoms.detail: A palpable or visible lesion on the penis, which may be nodular, ulcerative, or fungating.

symptoms.name: Penile pain
symptoms.detail: Pain may be associated with the lesion, especially with more advanced or complicated disease.

symptoms.name: Penile discharge or bleeding
symptoms.detail: Discharge (serous, purulent, or foul-smelling) or bleeding from the lesion.
symptoms.frequency: Common, particularly if ulcerated or infected.

symptoms.name: Foul odor
symptoms.detail: May occur from tissue necrosis or secondary infection, especially with delayed presentation.
symptoms.frequency: Less common, indicates advanced local disease.

symptoms.name: Palpable inguinal lymphadenopathy
symptoms.detail: Enlarged, palpable lymph nodes in the groin region.
symptoms.frequency: Present at diagnosis in a subset of patients; indicates regional spread.

symptoms.name: Constitutional symptoms
symptoms.frequency: May indicate advanced or metastatic disease.

symptoms.name: Phimosis
symptoms.detail: Tight foreskin that cannot be retracted over the glans. It is a significant risk factor and can obscure visualization of the primary tumor, delaying diagnosis.
symptoms.frequency: Present in 25% to 60% of penile cancer patients.

symptoms.name: Penile mass or ulceration
symptoms.detail: Direct visualization and palpation of the primary tumor on the penile shaft, glans, or prepuce.

symptoms.name: Inguinal lymphadenopathy
symptoms.detail: Palpable enlargement of inguinal lymph nodes. Note: 30% to 50% of palpable nodes are inflammatory, not metastatic.
symptoms.frequency: Present at diagnosis in a significant subset.

symptoms.name: Edema of penis/scrotum/legs
symptoms.detail: May occur with advanced disease causing lymphatic obstruction.
symptoms.frequency: Less common, indicates advanced local/regional disease.

alarms.name: Palpable inguinal lymphadenopathy
alarms.detail: Enlarged, palpable lymph nodes in the groin region.
